summary: I was born in Cortachy, Scotland in 1820. It’s a small place where my family lived before we decided to emigrate. Cortachy is where my story begins before we moved to Canada in 1836.

Scotland is where my roots are. I was born there in 1820 in Cortachy, and it’s also where Wilhelmina Schlaberg, whom I married in 1845, was from. We left Scotland in 1836 to start a new life in Canada.

After leaving Scotland in 1836, my family and I settled near LaChute, Canada. That’s where I married Wilhelmina Schlaberg and raised eleven children over twenty years. Although we built a life there, I wasn’t content and decided to seek new opportunities in America starting in 1867.

In 1867, my son James Jr. and I traveled to California to find land and establish a home for our family. We arrived in San Francisco after a long journey by ship and railroad. Unfortunately, the work was hard and my son fell ill, so after a few months we decided to return to Canada in early 1868.

After returning from California, I moved my family to the United States, first to Iowa where my wife’s brother had homesteaded. Later, we settled in Minnesota where I successfully homesteaded and became a citizen. This was the second attempt to make a home in America, and it was the one that lasted.

Iowa was the place my family moved to after returning from California and Canada. It was where my wife’s brother had already homesteaded, so it made sense for us to start there. This move marked the beginning of our successful life in America.

After living in Iowa, we eventually homesteaded in Minnesota. It was there that my children grew up, married, and started families of their own. My eldest son James Jr. married Anna Sheerin in Avoca, Minnesota in 1880, and from that union came my grandson George Lowe.

LaChute, Canada was where my family settled after emigrating from Scotland in 1836. It was near this place that I lived next to Wilhelmina Schlaberg’s family, whom I married in 1845. LaChute was home during those early years raising my children.

Wilhelmina Schlaberg was a fellow Scot who lived near us in LaChute, Canada. I married her in 1845, and together we raised eleven children over the next twenty years. She was a central part of my family’s life during our time in Canada and beyond.

I am James Lowe, born in Cortachy, Scotland in 1820. I emigrated with my family to Canada in 1836, married Wilhelmina Schlaberg, and raised eleven children. In 1867, I made my first attempt to establish a home in America by traveling to California with my son James Jr., but after hardships, we returned and eventually settled successfully in Iowa and Minnesota.

James Lowe Jr. was my eldest son who accompanied me on the journey to California in 1867. He became ill while we were working near Fort Ross and had to return to San Francisco to recover. Later, he married Anna Sheerin in 1880 in Avoca, Minnesota, and fathered my grandson George Lowe.

The Henry Chauncy was the ship my son James Jr. and I boarded in New York City in May 1867 to begin our journey to California. It took us south along the Atlantic coast to Panama, where we crossed by railroad before continuing by steamship to San Francisco. This ship marked the start of our challenging attempt to make a new life in America.

Panama was a key point in our journey to California in 1867. After sailing from New York on the Henry Chauncy, we crossed Panama by railroad before boarding the steamship Constitution to continue up the Pacific coast. It was a long and difficult trip that took 22 days to reach San Francisco.

After crossing Panama by railroad, my son and I boarded the steamship Constitution to sail up the Pacific coast to San Francisco. This leg of the journey completed our 22-day trip from New York in 1867. The voyage was tough, and I was sick from the sea when we arrived.

San Francisco was where my son and I arrived after our long journey in 1867. We found work there threshing on a farm before moving on to a sawmill near Fort Ross. It was also where James Jr. went to recover when he became ill, and from where we eventually departed to return to Canada in early 1868.

Fort Ross was near where I worked at a sawmill after arriving in California in 1867. The work was difficult and my son James Jr. fell ill during this time. It was a challenging winter, and eventually we decided to leave California and return to Canada.

The Dakota was the steamship we boarded in Panama in February 1868 to sail back to New York on our return journey from California. This marked the beginning of our trip back to Canada after the hardships we faced in California. It was part of the route that eventually brought me home in time to see my father before he died.

David Lowe was my father, whom I saw just before he died after returning from California in 1868. His passing was a significant moment for me and perhaps motivated my decision to move the family to Iowa shortly after. He was part of the family I left behind in Scotland and Canada before coming to America.

Anna Sheerin was the woman my eldest son, James Jr., married in 1880 in Avoca, Minnesota. Their marriage was part of the family’s successful establishment in America after our move from Canada and California. From their union came my grandson George Lowe.

In February 1868, my son and I sailed from San Francisco on the steamship America to Panama as part of our return journey to Canada. From Panama, we then boarded the Dakota to continue to New York. The America was a key vessel in our trip back after our difficult time in California.
